Since taking office in January, do you think Donald Trump has improperly used his office to enrich himself or his friends and family?
notes: Definitely done 40% + Probably done 20% = 60% support (has done). Probably NOT 19% + Definitely NOT 19% = 38% oppose.

question_wording: Since taking office in January, do you think Donald Trump has run an open and transparent administration?
notes: Definitely done 18% + Probably done 20% = 38% support. Probably NOT 20% + Definitely NOT 41% = 61% oppose.

question_wording: Since taking office in January, do you think Donald Trump has improved the way government works?
notes: Definitely done 19% + Probably done 20% = 39% support. Probably NOT 18% + Definitely NOT 41% = 59% oppose.

question_wording: Since taking office in January, do you think Donald Trump has improved the country's standing around the world?
notes: Definitely done 22% + Probably done 17% = 39% support. Probably NOT 18% + Definitely NOT 43% = 61% oppose.

question_wording: Since taking office in January, do you think Donald Trump has set a high moral standard for the presidency?
notes: Definitely done 16% + Probably done 19% = 35% support. Probably NOT 18% + Definitely NOT 46% = 64% oppose.

question_wording: Since taking office in January, do you think Donald Trump has improperly used his office to punish those who say things he does not like?
notes: Form 1 only. Definitely done 45% + Probably done 21% = 66%. Probably NOT 18% + Definitely NOT 15% = 33%.

question_wording: Since taking office in January, do you think Donald Trump has improperly encouraged federal investigations of his political opponents?
notes: Form 2 only. Definitely done 38% + Probably done 24% = 62%. Probably NOT 21% + Definitely NOT 15% = 36%.

question_wording: As you may know, Donald Trump has used a number of executive orders to set policy. Do you think Trump is doing too much, too little, or about the right amount by executive order?
notes: Form 2 only. Too much: 51%, Too little: 5%, About right: 27%, Not sure: 16%.

question_wording: Compared to other recent presidents, do you think Donald Trump is trying to exercise more presidential power?
notes: Form 1 only. More: 69%, Less: 2%, About the same: 21%, Not sure: 7%.

question_wording: And do you think Donald Trump trying to exercise MORE presidential power than other recent presidents is good or bad for the country?
notes: Form 1 only, among those saying Trump exercises more power. Good: 12%, Bad: 49%, Not sure: 7%.

question_wording: Some people say they are basically content with the federal government, others say they are frustrated and others say they are angry. Which of these best describes how you feel?
notes: Basically content: 23%, Frustrated: 49%, Angry: 26%. Oppose = Frustrated + Angry.

question_wording: How much of the time do you think you can trust the government in Washington to do what is right?
notes: Just about always: 2%, Most of the time: 15%, Only some of the time: 59%, Never: 24%. Support = Always + Most. Oppose = Only some + Never.

question_wording: Thinking about the possibility of violent extremism in the country, how much of a problem, if at all, is left-wing extremism for the country today?
notes: Major problem: 53%, Minor problem: 34%, Not a problem: 10%.

question_wording: Thinking about the possibility of violent extremism in the country, how much of a problem, if at all, is right-wing extremism for the country today?
notes: Major problem: 52%, Minor problem: 33%, Not a problem: 13%.

question_wording: Thinking about the possibility of violent extremism in the country, how much of a problem, if at all, is extremism from people without clear political views for the country today?
notes: Major problem: 47%, Minor problem: 38%, Not a problem: 13%.

question_wording: Do you think politically motivated violence in this country today is increasing, decreasing, or staying about the same?
notes: Form 1 only. Increasing: 85%, Decreasing: 3%, Staying about the same: 12%.

question_wording: In general, how comfortable do you think Republicans in this country are to freely and openly express their political views?
notes: Very comfortable: 40%, Somewhat comfortable: 34%, Not too comfortable: 18%, Not at all comfortable: 7%. Support = Very + Somewhat comfortable.

question_wording: In general, how comfortable do you think Democrats in this country are to freely and openly express their political views?
notes: Very comfortable: 36%, Somewhat comfortable: 34%, Not too comfortable: 22%, Not at all comfortable: 8%. Support = Very + Somewhat comfortable.

question_wording: Thinking about the United States, would you say the political system needs to be completely reformed, needs major changes, needs minor changes, or doesn't need to be changed?
notes: U.S. only. Completely reformed: 24%, Major changes: 52%, Minor changes: 19%, Doesn't need change: 3%. Support = complete reform + major changes.

question_wording: And how confident are you that the U.S. political system can be changed effectively - very confident, somewhat confident, not too confident, or not at all confident?
notes: U.S. only. Very confident: 7%, Somewhat confident: 28%, Not too confident: 44%, Not at all confident: 20%. Support = Very + Somewhat confident.

question_wording: Thinking about elected officials in the U.S., how many of them understand the needs of ordinary people?
notes: U.S. only. All/Most: 7% (All 1% + Most 6%), Some: 39%, Few/None: 54% (Few 47% + None 7%).

question_wording: Thinking about elected officials in the U.S., how many of them focus on the most important problems in the U.S.?
notes: U.S. only. All/Most: 9% (All 2% + Most 7%), Some: 42%, Few/None: 48% (Few 43% + None 5%).

question_wording: Thinking about elected officials in the U.S., how many of them are well-qualified for their positions?
notes: U.S. only. All/Most: 10% (All 1% + Most 9%), Some: 50%, Few/None: 40% (Few 35% + None 4%).

question_wording: Thinking about elected officials in the U.S., how many of them are ethical?
notes: U.S. only. All/Most: 7% (All 1% + Most 6%), Some: 44%, Few/None: 48% (Few 42% + None 5%).

question_wording: Thinking about elected officials in the U.S., how many of them are honest?
notes: U.S. only. All/Most: 7% (All 1% + Most 6%), Some: 40%, Few/None: 53% (Few 45% + None 8%).

question_wording: New polling conducted in February by Data for Progress and the Climate and Community Institute shows voters agree that climate and the economy are issues that can and should be addressed together : 7 in 10 voters believe it is possible for economic policy to lower costs while directly reducing emissions, compared with only 19% of voters who don’t think it’s possible.
notes: Article: Voters Support a Green Economic Populism Policy Agenda. Narrative polling finding.

question_wording: In a split sample test, which randomized whether respondents saw the phrase “issues like natural disasters, heat waves, and prolonged droughts” or “climate change,” 80% of respondents say extreme weather events somewhat or greatly impact the rising cost of living, compared with only 61% who say the same about climate change.
notes: Article: Voters Support a Green Economic Populism Policy Agenda. Narrative polling finding.

question_wording: Voters are experiencing the rising cost of living across the board, with 86% of respondents facing higher grocery costs and 82% facing higher electricity and gas costs.
notes: Article: Voters Support a Green Economic Populism Policy Agenda. Narrative polling finding.

question_wording: At least 3 in 5 voters also report higher health care (68%) and home insurance costs (60%).
notes: Article: Voters Support a Green Economic Populism Policy Agenda. Narrative polling finding.

question_wording: In the next year, at least 7 in 10 voters expect to pay more for groceries (79%), home electricity and gas (77%), and health care (70%), while 63% of voters expect to see rising home insurance costs.

question_wording: An additional 20% say they do not associate climate change with the rising cost of living at all, while 14% blame “corporate pricing decisions.” Policy preferences around the cost of living and climate While there may not be consensus among voters around the main drivers of rising prices associated with climate change, 7 in 10 voters agree that climate interventions can reduce the cost of living.
notes: Article: Voters Support a Green Economic Populism Policy Agenda. Narrative polling finding.

question_wording: Furthermore, a majority of voters (55%) would prefer that elected officials address both the cost of living and climate change through coordinated policies, while 35% want to see elected officials focus primarily on the cost of living and 4% say the same about climate change.

question_wording: A similar majority of voters (56%) think that the government should “expand its involvement” in the issue of climate change and the cost of living via new policies, investments, and regulations.

question_wording: Only 26% think the government should “reduce its involvement” by defunding and deregulating climate policies and relying more on the private sector to lower costs.

question_wording: More specifically, voters consider it important for elected officials to take action on key economic and climate priorities, including nearly universal agreement about the importance of lowering the cost of living (96%) and protecting people and their homes from natural disasters (91%).
notes: Article: Voters Support a Green Economic Populism Policy Agenda. Narrative polling finding.

question_wording: Voters also view ensuring the affordability of home insurance (89%), the reduction of harmful pollution (88%), the strength of the public sector (84%), and environmental sustainability (82%) as critically important priorities.
notes: Article: Voters Support a Green Economic Populism Policy Agenda. Narrative polling finding.

question_wording: Below are the key findings from this research: Latino Voters Are Motivated to Elect a President Who Will Take Extreme Weather and Climate Change Seriously and Hold Big Oil Polluters Accountable Latino voters prioritize addressing climate change and extreme weather, with 65% of respondents agreeing with a statement saying the U.S.
notes: Article: Latino Voters Want a President Who Will Address Costs, Causes of Extreme Weather. Narrative polling finding.

notes: Article: Latino Voters Want a President Who Will Address Costs, Causes of Extreme Weather. Narrative polling finding.

notes: Article: Latino Voters Want a President Who Will Address Costs, Causes of Extreme Weather. Narrative polling finding.

question_wording: This includes majorities of Latino voters in key swing states (73%), as well as Latino voters who identify as Democrats (86%), Independents (71%), and Republicans (70%).
notes: Article: Latino Voters Want a President Who Will Address Costs, Causes of Extreme Weather. Narrative polling finding.

question_wording: Over three-quarters of Latino voters report that extreme weather events impact their energy bills (78%) and electricity bills (78%), highlighting that Latino voters think extreme weather causes similar levels of impact, regardless of which term is used.
notes: Article: Latino Voters Want a President Who Will Address Costs, Causes of Extreme Weather. Narrative polling finding.

question_wording: In addition to a general increase in energy costs, Latino voters report home heating and cooling costs (77%) and their ability to enjoy the outdoors (74%) are also somewhat or greatly impacted by extreme weather.
notes: Article: Latino Voters Want a President Who Will Address Costs, Causes of Extreme Weather. Narrative polling finding.

notes: Article: Latino Voters Want a President Who Will Address Costs, Causes of Extreme Weather. Narrative polling finding.

question_wording: Data for Progress conducted a new survey to gauge attitudes toward PFAS, finding that 71% of likely voters have heard nothing at all about the term.
notes: Article: Voters Are Deeply Concerned About Health Impacts of PFAS and Support Creating a Fund for Groups Impacted by Exposure. Narrative polling finding.

question_wording: The related term “forever chemicals” is still unfamiliar to 50% of voters, demonstrating that the salience of these issues remains low overall.
notes: Article: Voters Are Deeply Concerned About Health Impacts of PFAS and Support Creating a Fund for Groups Impacted by Exposure. Narrative polling finding.

question_wording: After reading this, a strong bipartisan majority of respondents (72%) say that the federal government is not providing enough access to information and resources on PFAS.
notes: Article: Voters Are Deeply Concerned About Health Impacts of PFAS and Support Creating a Fund for Groups Impacted by Exposure. Narrative polling finding.

question_wording: When asked what, if any, health problems they associate with PFAS or forever chemicals, respondents most commonly cite kidney, liver, prostate, and testicular cancer (34%).
notes: Article: Voters Are Deeply Concerned About Health Impacts of PFAS and Support Creating a Fund for Groups Impacted by Exposure. Narrative polling finding.

question_wording: Respondents also associate PFAS with developmental effects (33%), followed closely by reproductive harm (32%), and lower immune system response (29%).
notes: Article: Voters Are Deeply Concerned About Health Impacts of PFAS and Support Creating a Fund for Groups Impacted by Exposure. Narrative polling finding.

question_wording: Nearly 1 in 5 respondents (18%) say they do not associate any of the health problems listed with PFAS, and over a quarter say they have not heard of either PFAS or forever chemicals (26%).
notes: Article: Voters Are Deeply Concerned About Health Impacts of PFAS and Support Creating a Fund for Groups Impacted by Exposure. Narrative polling finding.

question_wording: Three-quarters (75%) of respondents say they support the new PFAS drinking water standards, with the rule receiving strong bipartisan support.
notes: Article: Voters Are Deeply Concerned About Health Impacts of PFAS and Support Creating a Fund for Groups Impacted by Exposure. Narrative polling finding.

question_wording: Moreover, around one-third of voters say that industry and chemical companies should be held most responsible for paying to clean up municipal water systems to meet the new drinking water standards (36%).
notes: Article: Voters Are Deeply Concerned About Health Impacts of PFAS and Support Creating a Fund for Groups Impacted by Exposure. Narrative polling finding.

question_wording: On average, respondents estimate that around 65% of Americans have PFAS in their bloodstream.
notes: Article: Voters Are Deeply Concerned About Health Impacts of PFAS and Support Creating a Fund for Groups Impacted by Exposure. Narrative polling finding.

question_wording: Later, respondents read that an estimated 98% of Americans have some level of PFAS in their bloodstream, that some forms of PFAS can take several years to exit the body, and that PFAS exposure is linked to serious health issues, including cancers and hormone disruption.
notes: Article: Voters Are Deeply Concerned About Health Impacts of PFAS and Support Creating a Fund for Groups Impacted by Exposure. Narrative polling finding.

question_wording: More than 8 in 10 voters (83%) respond that they are concerned about long-term exposure to PFAS, with over half saying that they are very concerned (51%).
notes: Article: Voters Are Deeply Concerned About Health Impacts of PFAS and Support Creating a Fund for Groups Impacted by Exposure. Narrative polling finding.

question_wording: More than three-quarters of respondents (78%) report that they are at least somewhat concerned about their own exposure to PFAS in the food supply.
notes: Article: Voters Are Deeply Concerned About Health Impacts of PFAS and Support Creating a Fund for Groups Impacted by Exposure. Narrative polling finding.

question_wording: Voters are most concerned about PFAS exposure via direct consumption, including through drinking water (69%) and food or food packaging (56%).
notes: Article: Voters Are Deeply Concerned About Health Impacts of PFAS and Support Creating a Fund for Groups Impacted by Exposure. Narrative polling finding.

question_wording: Notably, among the listed routes of exposure, voters express the least concern about firefighting foam, also known as AFFF (5%), despite the fact that AFFF is one of the largest sources of PFAS contamination in drinking water.
notes: Article: Voters Are Deeply Concerned About Health Impacts of PFAS and Support Creating a Fund for Groups Impacted by Exposure. Narrative polling finding.

question_wording: Similarly, few respondents (9%) share concerns about exposure from biosolids, often broadly referred to as municipal sludge, which is a significant carrier of PFAS.
notes: Article: Voters Are Deeply Concerned About Health Impacts of PFAS and Support Creating a Fund for Groups Impacted by Exposure. Narrative polling finding.

question_wording: Among voters who indicate they would support the establishment of a fund for groups adversely impacted by PFAS contamination, 60% think that both federal and state governments should be responsible for creating such a fund.
notes: Article: Voters Are Deeply Concerned About Health Impacts of PFAS and Support Creating a Fund for Groups Impacted by Exposure. Narrative polling finding.

question_wording: About a quarter of voters (27%) think the federal government alone should be responsible for creating a fund, while 12% think only individual state governments should be responsible.
notes: Article: Voters Are Deeply Concerned About Health Impacts of PFAS and Support Creating a Fund for Groups Impacted by Exposure. Narrative polling finding.

notes: Article: Voters Are Largely Unaware of the Discrimination Faced by Little People. Narrative polling finding.

question_wording: New polling from Data for Progress shows that the administration is not alone in these concerns, with 84% of likely voters expressing that they are somewhat or very concerned about workers facing extreme heat while at the workplace.
